title: Workbook — The Holy Spirit
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class, in a group, or for personal study. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. Who is the Holy Spirit? Is he a force, an energy, an influence — or a divine Person?
  2. What does the name «Paraclete» mean, and why does Jesus use it to describe the Holy Spirit?
  3. Name the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit as listed in Isaiah 11:2-3 and the Catholic tradition. For each one, write a single sentence explaining what it does in the soul.
  4. What happened on the morning of Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4)? List the three signs — the sound, the sight, and the effect — and explain the significance of each.
  5. According to Romans 8:26-27, what does the Holy Spirit do when we are unable to pray? Why is this one of the most comforting promises in all of Scripture?
  6. What is the difference between the gifts of the Holy Spirit (given for personal sanctification) and charisms (given for the building up of the Church)? Give one example of each.

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly and thoughtfully. There are no "correct" answers to these personal questions — only honest ones.

1. Before reading this lesson, what image or idea did you have of the Holy Spirit? Was it a vague concept, a dove, a feeling, a force? How has your understanding changed now that you know he is a divine Person who dwells within you — with intelligence, will, and the capacity to love?

Take your time. Write freely. Sometimes the best way to discover what you really believe is to put it on paper.

 

 

 

 

2. Of the seven gifts (wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety, and fear of the Lord), which one do you feel you need most right now in your life? Think of a specific situation — a decision, a struggle, a relationship — and explain why that gift would make a difference.

Do not just name the gift. Describe the concrete situation where you need it, and imagine what it would look like if the Holy Spirit gave you that gift in abundance.

 

 

 

 

 

3. The fruits of the Spirit are «love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control» (Gal 5:22-23). Do a personal inventory: put a ✓ next to the three fruits you see most in your life, and a ? next to the three that need the most growth. For one of the «?» fruits, describe a situation this week where you could practice it.

Be brave enough to be honest. The point is not to feel bad about yourself but to know where to focus your prayer and effort.

 

 

 

 

 

 

4. Romans 8:26 says the Spirit «intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words.» Think of a time when you were in so much pain, confusion, or darkness that you could not pray — the words would not come, or you did not even want to pray. Looking back, can you see signs that Someone was holding you and praying in you? What does it change to know you were never praying alone?

This question may touch something tender. That's okay. Write what is true, even if it is hard.

 

 

 

 

 

 

5. If you truly believed that your body is a «temple of the Holy Spirit» (1 Cor 6:19) — that the living God dwells within you, here and now — what is one concrete decision you would make this week to honor that temple and give the Spirit more room to act?

Be specific. Not «I will be a better person,» but something like: «I will spend five minutes in silence before bed, inviting the Spirit to speak,» or «I will forgive my brother and tell him so,» or «I will turn off my phone for an hour and pay attention to the people in front of me.»

 

 

 

 

 

6. At Pentecost, fearful apostles became bold witnesses. What is the biggest fear holding you back from living and sharing your faith more openly? Ask the Holy Spirit directly for the gift of fortitude — and write down what you think he might be saying to you in response.

Fear is not a sign of weak faith. The apostles had faith and were still afraid — until the Spirit filled them. Name your fear, lay it before the Spirit, and listen.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Fruit Inventory

Write out the list of the fruits of the Spirit from Galatians 5:22-23. Next to each one, place a symbol: ✓ if you see this fruit growing in your life, ? if you are not sure, ✗ if you feel it needs urgent attention. Then choose ONE fruit marked ✗ or ? and write a specific, measurable action you will take this week to cultivate it — with the Spirit's help.

 

 

 

 

 

 

B. Meditate on the «Veni Sancte Spiritus» Sequence

Find the text of the «Veni Sancte Spiritus» sequence online (it is the Pentecost sequence from the Roman Missal). Read it slowly, aloud if possible. Pause after each verse. Underline or copy out the three phrases that touch you most deeply. What are they asking of the Holy Spirit? Write a short paragraph: why did those particular phrases stand out to you today?

 

 

 

 

 

C. Daily Invocation to the Holy Spirit

For one week, before every significant decision — before an exam, before a difficult conversation, before making a plan, before responding to someone who has hurt you — pause, breathe, and say (out loud or in your heart): «Come, Holy Spirit.» Keep a simple log below:

Day Situation What happened after?
   
   
   
   
   

D. Read Acts 2:1-41 — The Full Pentecost Account

Read Acts 2:1-41 in one sitting. It takes about five minutes. Then, using your imagination and the text, answer these questions: what did the room smell like? What sounds surrounded the apostles? How did Peter's voice sound different from what we know of him before? What would you have done if you were in the crowd that day? Write a paragraph describing your imagined experience.

 

 

 

 

 

 

E. Discover Your Charisms

Take five minutes of silence. Ask the Holy Spirit to show you what gifts he has given you to serve others — not the spectacular ones necessarily, but the real ones. Write down whatever thoughts, memories, or images come to mind. What do people thank you for? What comes naturally to you that others find hard? What kind of service gives you deep joy? These are clues to your charisms.

 

 

 

 

 

 

F. Study a Symbol

The Catechism lists multiple symbols of the Holy Spirit: water, anointing, fire, cloud and light, the seal, the hand, the finger, and the dove (CCC 694-701). Choose the symbol that resonates most with you right now. Find the corresponding CCC paragraphs. Read them. Then draw the symbol on a piece of paper and, around it, write words and phrases that express what the Spirit means to you through that symbol.

 

 

 

 

Memory Verse

«The Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know how to pray as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words. And he who searches the hearts knows what is the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to the will of God.»

— Romans 8:26-27

Tip for memorizing: Read it aloud three times. Then cover the text and try to say it from memory. Repeat once a day. By the end of the week, it will be yours.

Prayer for This Week

Come, Holy Spirit,
fill the heart of your faithful
and kindle in them the fire of your love.

Come, father of the poor,
come, giver of gifts,
come, light of hearts.

You are the best consoler,
the soul's sweet guest,
refreshment in the heat of the day.

In labor, you are rest;
in the scorching sun, you are the shade;
in tears, you are comfort.

O most blessed Light,
fill the deepest places of my heart.
Without your strength, nothing in us is strong,
nothing is holy.

Wash what is unclean,
water what is dry,
heal what is wounded.

Bend what is rigid,
warm what is cold,
guide what has gone astray.

Give to your faithful
who trust in you
your seven holy gifts.

Give us the reward of virtue,
give us the salvation that has no end,
give us joy that lasts forever.
Amen. Alleluia.

— Adapted from the «Veni Sancte Spiritus» Sequence

Print Workbook
